narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 31. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past students' academic and background details, estimated your probability of failing at 45.8%—a risk level that unfortunately tipped the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on how your specific achievements stack up against patterns seen in thousands of similar students. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since your 3.10 is reasonably close to the dataset average of 3.21, showing solid college performance. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1 also nudged the model toward expecting failure, as it similarly positioned you at the lower end compared to peers' averages near the 6th decile. Overall, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students (over 90%), these early and later academic deciles carried the heaviest weight, outweighing strengths like your GPA in the model's eyes. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank higher in school deciles and score around 37 on the LSAT, your profile signals higher bar exam risk to the system. This doesn't reflect your full potential or effort—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying next cycle with these targeted improvements could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value your unique path.
